title: What Did I Lose When I Lost Them?
subtitle: A journal prompt for mapping the full shape of a loss
excerpt: A journal prompt for mapping what was lost alongside the person: the conversations, the rituals, the version of yourself that existed in relation to them. The prompt asks for specifics, not a summary of grief.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you want to map the full texture of a loss rather than hold it as one undifferentiated weight. Not for acute, raw grief — for grief that has settled enough to be examined.

content.text: A ten-minute journal prompt for the specifics of a loss. Grief is easier to hold when its shape is named. This prompt asks for the particular things, not the general feeling.
New to journal prompts as a practice? Read

Write about what you lost when you lost them. Include the specific things: the conversations, the rituals, the version of yourself that existed in relation to them. What did you expect to do with them that will not now happen? What went with them that nobody else can replace?

Write without editing. Name the small things alongside the large ones. A particular phrase they used, a thing you only did together, a question you cannot now ask. The more specific the writing, the more useful it tends to be.
